tex:
(a) The table shows the areas, in km$^2$, of the four largest rainforests in the world.
\newline

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}
        \begin{tabularx}{0.8\textwidth}{|l|r|}
        \hline
        Rainforest & Area (km$^2$) \\
        \hline
        Amazon & $5\,500\,000$ \\
        \hline
        Congo & $2\,000\,000$ \\
        \hline
        Atlantic & $1\,315\,000$ \\
        \hline
        Valdivian & $250\,000$ \\
        \hline
        \end{tabularx}
    \end{InsertScreenShot}

    (i) (b) Find the area of the Valdivian rainforest as a percentage of the area of the Amazon rainforest.
\newline

        

(c) Write, in its simplest form, the ratio of the areas of the rainforests Valdivian : Atlantic : Congo.
\newline

         :  : 

(d) The Amazon rainforest has 60\% of its area in Brazil and 10\% of its area in Colombia.
\newline
 \\
        $\dfrac{1}{43}$\% of the remaining area of the rainforest is in Peru.
\newline
 \\
        Find the percentage of the Amazon rainforest that is in Brazil, Colombia and Peru.
\newline

        

(e) The area of the Amazon rainforest represents $\dfrac{27}{50}$ of the total area of rainforest in the world.
\newline
 \\
        Calculate the total area of rainforest in the world.
\newline
 \\
        Give your answer correct to the nearest $100\,000$ km$^2$.
\newline

        

(f) In the world, 60.7 hectares of rainforest are lost every minute.
\newline
 \\
        Calculate the total area, in hectares, of rainforest that is lost in 365 days.
\newline
 \\
        Give your answer in standard form.
\newline

        

(g) The Amazon river has a length of 6440 km, correct to the nearest 10 km.
\newline
 \\
    The Congo river has a length of 4400 km, correct to the nearest 100 km.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate the upper bound of the difference between the lengths of the Amazon river and the Congo river.
\newline

tex:
(a) On the grid, draw the image of

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}

    \end{InsertScreenShot}

    (i) (b) triangle T after a reflection in the x-axis

(c) triangle T after a translation by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} -5 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$

(d) triangle T after an enlargement by scale factor $\dfrac{1}{2}$ with centre $(-1, 1)$

(e) A shape P is enlarged by scale factor 3 to give shape Q.
\newline
 \\
    Shape Q is then enlarged by scale factor $\dfrac{2}{5}$ to give shape R.
\newline
 \\
    The area of shape P is 10 cm$^2$.

 \\

content.content: On the grid, draw the image of
triangle T after a reflection in the x-axis
triangle T after a translation by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} -5 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$
triangle T after an enlargement by scale factor $\dfrac{1}{2}$ with centre $(-1, 1)$

A shape P is enlarged by scale factor 3 to give shape Q.
\newline
 \\
    Shape Q is then enlarged by scale factor $\dfrac{2}{5}$ to give shape R.
\newline
 \\
    The area of shape P is 10 cm$^2$.
\newline
 \\
    Calculate the area of shape R.
\newline

qtype: substitution, solving quadratic equations, algebraic fractions, expanding and simplifying algebraic expressions, simplifying expressions with indices
tex:
(a) $C = \dfrac{1}{4}xy^2$

    (i) (b) Find $C$ when $x = 5$ and $y = 8$.
\newline

        

(c) Find the positive value of $y$ when $C = 15$ and $x = 2.4$.
\newline

        

(d) Write as a single fraction in its simplest form.
\newline
 \\
    $\dfrac{4}{x - 1} - \dfrac{3}{2x + 5}$

    

(e) Expand and simplify.
\newline
 \\
    $(2x + 3)^4 - x^2$

    

(f) Simplify.
\newline
 \\
    $\dfrac{-43y^8}{16} \times \dfrac{16x}{p}$

content.content: $C = \dfrac{1}{4}xy^2$

Find $C$ when $x = 5$ and $y = 8$.
\newline

Find the positive value of $y$ when $C = 15$ and $x = 2.4$.
\newline

Write as a single fraction in its simplest form.
\newline
 \\
    $\dfrac{4}{x - 1} - \dfrac{3}{2x + 5}$

Expand and simplify.
\newline
 \\
    $(2x + 3)^4 - x^2$

Simplify.
\newline
 \\
    $\dfrac{-43y^8}{16} \times \dfrac{16x}{p}$

tex:
(a) Jianyu records the time, in seconds, that some cars take to travel 195 m.
\newline
 \\
    The box and whisker plot shows this information.
\newline

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}

    \end{InsertScreenShot}

    (i) (b) Find the median time.
\newline

        

(c) Find the interquartile range.
\newline

        

(d) Find the difference between the average speed of the fastest car and the average speed of the \\
        slowest car.
\newline
 \\
        Give your answer in kilometres per hour.
\newline

        

(e) Matilda records the distances that 80 different cars can travel with a full tank of fuel.
\newline
 \\
    The table shows this information.
\newline

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}
        \begin{tabularx}{0.9\textwidth}{|c|c|}
        \hline
        Distance ($d$ km) & Frequency \\
        \hline
        $250 \leq d < 300$ & 7 \\
        \hline
        $300 \leq d < 400$ & 13 \\
        \hline
        $400 \leq d < 420$ & 19 \\
        \hline
        $420 \leq d < 450$ & 21 \\
        \hline
        $450 \leq d < 500$ & 20 \\
        \hline
        \end{tabularx}
    \end{InsertScreenShot}

    (i) (f) Write down the class interval that contains the median.
\newline

        

(g) Calculate an estimate of the mean.
\newline

        

(h) A histogram is drawn to show the information in the table.
\newline
 \\
        The height of the bar for the interval $250 \leq d < 300$ is 2.8 cm.
\newline
 \\
        Calculate the height of the bar for each of the following intervals.
\newline

        $300 \leq d < 400$:  \\
        $400 \leq d < 420$:  \\
        $420 \leq d < 450$: 

(i) Two of the 80 cars are chosen at random.
\newline
 \\
        Find the probability that, with a full tank of fuel, one of the cars can travel more than 450 km \\
        and the other car can travel not more than 300 km.
\newline

tex:
The diagram shows the positions of two lighthouses A and B, a boat C and a harbour H.
\newline
 \\
C is due east of B.
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}
    % Navigation diagram: Lighthouses A and B, boat C (due east of B), harbour H
    % Distances: AB=11km, BC=14km, BH=32km
    % Angles: At B: 25°, At H: 55°
    % (NOT TO SCALE)
\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) Find the bearing of the harbour from boat C.
\newline

    

(b) (i) Show that angle CBH = 100°.
\newline

    

    (ii) Show that BH = 13.7 km, correct to 1 decimal place.
\newline

    

(c) Calculate the bearing of A from B.
\newline

    

(d) At 1 pm boat C sails 32 km directly to the harbour at a speed of 10 knots.
\newline

    (i) Calculate the time when boat C arrives at the harbour.
\newline
 \\
    Give this time correct to the nearest minute.
\newline
 \\
    {[1 knot = 1.852 km/h]}

    

    (ii) Calculate the distance of boat C to the harbour when boat C is at the shortest distance from \\
    lighthouse B.
\newline

content.content:

    \Marks{1}

\newline

Calculate the bearing of A from B.

tex:
(a) The diagram shows a box in the shape of a cuboid.
\newline
 \\
    The box is open at the top.
\newline

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}

    \end{InsertScreenShot}

    (i) (b) Work out the surface area of the inside of the open box.
\newline

        

(c) Cylinders with height 20 cm and diameter 15 cm are placed in the box.
\newline
 \\
        Work out the maximum number of these cylinders that can completely fit inside the box.
\newline

        

(d) A solid bronze cone has a mass 750 g.
\newline
 \\
    The density of the bronze is 8.9 g/cm$^3$.
\newline
 \\
    The ratio radius of cone : height of cone = 1 : 3.
\newline

    (i) (e) Show that the radius of the cone is 2.99 cm, correct to 3 significant figures.
\newline
 \\
        {[Density = mass $\div$ volume]} \\
        $\left[\text{The volume, } V\text{, of a cone with radius } r \text{ and height } h \text{ is } V = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h.\right]$

        

(f) Calculate the total surface area of the cone.
\newline
 \\
        $\left[\text{The curved surface area, } A\text{, of a cone with radius } r \text{ and slant height } l \text{ is } A = \pi rl.\right]$

content.content:
The diagram shows a box in the shape of a cuboid.
\newline
 \\
    The box is open at the top.
\newline

Work out the surface area of the inside of the open box.
\newline

Cylinders with height 20 cm and diameter 15 cm are placed in the box.
\newline
 \\
        Work out the maximum number of these cylinders that can completely fit inside the box.
\newline

A solid bronze cone has a mass 750 g.
\newline
 \\
    The density of the bronze is 8.9 g/cm$^3$.
\newline
 \\
    The ratio radius of cone : height of cone = 1 : 3.
\newline

Show that the radius of the cone is 2.99 cm, correct to 3 significant figures.
\newline
 \\
        {[Density = mass $\div$ volume]} \\
        $\left[\text{The volume, } V\text{, of a cone with radius } r \text{ and height } h \text{ is } V = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h.\right]$

Calculate the total surface area of the cone.
\newline
 \\
        $\left[\text{The curved surface area, } A\text{, of a cone with radius } r \text{ and slant height } l \text{ is } A = \pi rl.\right]$

qtype: sketching quadratic graphs, finding intercepts, differentiation, finding turning points, solving simultaneous linear and quadratic equations
tex:
(a) On the axes, sketch the graph of $y = x^2 + 7x - 18$.
\newline
 \\
    On your sketch, write the values where the graph meets the x-axis and the y-axis.
\newline

    \begin{InsertScreenShot}
        % Graph of y = x² + 7x - 18
        % X-intercepts: (-9, 0) and (2, 0)
        % Y-intercept: (0, -18)
        % (NOT TO SCALE)
    \end{InsertScreenShot}

(b) (i) Find the derivative of $y = x^2 - 3x - 28$.
\newline

    

    (ii) Find the coordinates of the turning point of $y = x^2 - 3x - 28$.
\newline

    

(c) The line $y = 5 - 2x$ intersects the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 28$ at point P and point Q.
\newline
 \\
    Find the coordinates of P and Q.
\newline
 \\
    You must show all your working and give your answers correct to 2 decimal places.
\newline

     \\

qtype: function evaluation, composite functions, inverse functions, solving functional equations
tex:
$f(x) = 4x + 1 \quad g(x) = 6 - 2x \quad h(x) = 3^x - 2$

(a) Find

    (i) (b) $f(3)$

        

(c) $gf(3)$.
\newline

        

(d) Find $g^{-1}(x)$.
\newline

    

(e) Find $x$ when $f(x) = g(2x - 7)$.
\newline

    

(f) Find the value of $hh(2)$.
\newline

    

(g) Find $x$ when $h^{-1}(x) = 10$.
\newline

content.content: Find

$gf(3)$.
\newline

Find $g^{-1}(x)$.
\newline

Find $x$ when $f(x) = g(2x - 7)$.
\newline

Find the value of $hh(2)$.
\newline

Find $x$ when $h^{-1}(x) = 10$.
\newline
